abstract:
This study provides a comparative analysis of copyright law across three major global jurisdictions: the European Union (EU), the United States (US), and the People’s Republic of China (China). By mapping the fundamental similarities and differences between these legal regimes, the research investigates the drivers behind the observed patterns of divergence and convergence. My findings suggest that international harmonization efforts, particularly the strictures of the Berne Convention, account for many shared legal features. In the case of China, economic motivations and external trade pressures have led to a copyright framework that is now remarkably similar to those of the US and EU, despite a vastly different historical and cultural heritage.

Nevertheless, significant differences persist, primarily rooted in the theoretical foundations of copyright doctrine. While the US maintains a largely utilitarian approach, the EU tends to emphasize "moral rights" stemming from the author’s personality. The traditional divide between common-law and civil-law systems also plays a role in how statutory law and judicial precedent are utilized. Interestingly, while China is structurally a civil-law system, its underlying rationale for protection and its practical reliance on precedent share surprising commonalities with the US common-law approach. Conversely, China aligns more closely with the EU regarding the statutory recognition of moral rights. Ultimately, this study illustrates how global legal transplants and domestic institutional needs continue to reshape the boundaries of intellectual property.
